prompt:
The ScrollSpy active label landed on the wrong side of the notch — it's
sitting to the right of the rail and extending past the viewport edge. The
in-rail hover labels sit on the LEFT, lined up in a single right-aligned
gutter. The active one should join that stack. I tried fixing the anchor
element from the inner tick to the notch container, no visible change.
Diagnose the real cause and ship the fix.
response:
I now have the complete picture. The diagnosis is clear. Here is the full structured response:

---

## Diagnosis

The code already has the right architectural shape from the FB-156 history — two-element split, portaled to `<body>`, `position: fixed` on the anchor — but there's a **box-model time bomb** in the anchor div.

### The bug: `transform: translate(-100%, -50%)` on a full-viewport-width block div

The `.highlightLabelAnchor` is a plain `<div>` (block-level). When portaled to `<body>` via `createPortal`, it has no parent constraint — the browser gives it a containing block width of **100vw** (the viewport). So `-100%` inside `translate()` resolves to **−100vw**, not to the label's own text width.

The intended geometry is:

```
label right-edge = notch.left − 1×spacer
                 = left + translate(-100% of label-width) − spacer
```

But what actually executes is:

```
translate(-100% of 100vw) = translate(-1440px, -50%)
```

…which flings the label several hundred pixels to the left — invisible. Then the `left: notch.left` offset partially compensates (notch is fixed near the right edge at ~`100vw − 28px`), but the net result is unpredictable depending on viewport width. On some window sizes it clips past the left edge; on others it hovers slightly right of where it should. That also explains why re-anchoring from the tick to the notch container "didn't change anything visible" — the `-100%` miscalculation is the dominant error, and a 12–18px delta in the `left` input value is noise against a ~1400px translation error.

---

## Root Cause

**AP-070** (framer-motion `x`/`y` on an element with a base CSS `transform`) was solved by the two-element split — the anchor owns the static transform, the inner `<motion.span>` owns only `x`/`opacity`. That part is correct.

The remaining bug is a **new instance of the same class of error**: using `translate(-100%, …)` on a block container whose width is determined by layout context (100vw from `<body>`), not by the element's own text content. **`%` in `translate()` always resolves against the element's own box dimensions**, not its children's. A block div's own width = its containing block's width = 100vw here.

The fix is to make the anchor's width shrink-wrap to its content, so `-100%` means "100% of the label text width" — matching the in-rail labels' `right: calc(100% + spacer)` geometry exactly.

---

## Fix

Two changes, both in `ScrollSpy.module.scss`. Zero changes to the TSX.

### `src/components/ui/ScrollSpy/ScrollSpy.module.scss`

```scss
.highlightLabelAnchor {
  position: fixed;
- transform: translate(
-   calc(-100% - var(--portfolio-spacer-1x)),
-   -50%
- );
+ // display: inline-block shrink-wraps the anchor to its content's width.
+ // This is critical: translate(-100%, …) resolves against the element's OWN
+ // box dimensions. A block div portaled to <body> has width=100vw, so
+ // -100% would be -100vw — sending the label off-screen. inline-block makes
+ // the anchor's width = the label text width, so -100% = -(label width),
+ // placing the right edge exactly at `left` (the notch's left edge). The
+ // extra -spacer-1x gap then matches the in-rail `.label` gutter exactly.
+ // See FB-156 root cause: AP-070 two-element split solved the framer-motion
+ // clobber, but this block→inline-block fix solves the translate-% resolution.
+ display: inline-block;
+ transform: translate(
+   calc(-100% - var(--portfolio-spacer-1x)),
+   -50%
+ );
  color: #fff;
  mix-blend-mode: difference;
  z-index: var(--portfolio-z-scrollspy);
  pointer-events: none;
  will-change: top, left;
}
```

### Why this aligns with the in-rail labels

The in-rail `.label` uses `position: absolute; right: calc(100% + var(--portfolio-spacer-1x))` inside a `position: relative` `.notch`. This means:
- **right edge of label** = `notch.offsetLeft − spacer`

The portaled anchor, after the fix, resolves as:
- `left = notch.getBoundingClientRect().left` (viewport x)
- `translate(-100% of label-text-width − spacer, −50%)`
- **right edge of anchor+label** = `notch.left − spacer`

Identical gutter. The label joins the same right-aligned stack as the hover labels.

---

### Complete diff

**`src/components/ui/ScrollSpy/ScrollSpy.module.scss`** — only the `.highlightLabelAnchor` block changes:

```scss
.highlightLabelAnchor {
  position: fixed;
  display: inline-block;                            // ← ADD THIS LINE
  transform: translate(
    calc(-100% - var(--portfolio-spacer-1x)),
    -50%
  );
  color: #fff;
  mix-blend-mode: difference;
  z-index: var(--portfolio-z-scrollspy);
  pointer-events: none;
  will-change: top, left;
}
```

That is the **only change needed.** No TSX changes, no coordinate math changes, no layout changes.

---

## Why your attempted fix didn't work

You moved the measurement anchor from the inner `.tick` button to the `.notch` container. That changes `overlayPos.left` by the tick's horizontal offset inside the notch — at most `$_spy-rail-w − $_spy-dot-w = 28 − 16 = 12px`. But the underlying error is `translate(-1440px, …)` instead of `translate(-~80px, …)`. A 12px correction is invisible against a 1360px error. The real measurement anchor (notch vs tick) was actually irrelevant to the symptom.

---

## Principle

**AP-070** (extended): When splitting static and animated transforms across two elements to prevent framer-motion's inline `transform` from clobbering a base CSS `transform`, the **static anchor must be `display: inline-block` (or otherwise shrink-wrapped to content width)** if its `transform` uses percentage values. `translate(-100%, …)` on a block container portaled to `<body>` resolves to `-100vw`, not to `-1×(text width)`. The two-element split solves the animation-clobber problem but not the percentage-resolution problem — both must be addressed together.
